title: Domain-Driven Design
subtitle: Tackling Complexity in the Heart of Software
author: Eric Evans
description: The foundational text that coined the term 'domain-driven design.' Evans argues that for complex software domains, the domain model — not the framework, not the database schema, not the UI — must be the heart of the design. Through a blend of strategic and tactical patterns, he shows how persistent, creative collaboration between developers and domain experts produces software models that encode genuine business insight. Unusually for a technical book, it is as much about language and communication as it is about code.
keyIdeas: The domain model must be the heart of the software design — not the database, not the framework
Ubiquitous Language: a shared, rigorously used language that connects developers and domain experts and appears in code, documents, and conversation
Bounded Contexts: every model has a boundary; within it, the model is consistent; between contexts, translation is required
Strategic design vs. tactical design: large systems need both — large-scale mapping and fine-grained model elements
Entities, Value Objects, Aggregates: the three core tactical building blocks, each serving a distinct conceptual role
Repositories and Factories: the two standard mechanisms for accessing and creating domain objects without exposing persistence details
Anti-Corruption Layer: a protective isolation boundary that prevents an external model from contaminating your own

Context Maps: explicit diagrams and agreements that show how bounded contexts relate to each other (Partnership, Shared Kernel, Customer/Supplier, Conformist, Anticorruption Layer, Open-host Service, Published Language, Separate Ways)
The domain expert is not a passive source of requirements — they are a co-creator of the model
CRUD-only architectures fail for complex domains because they encode no behavior, no invariants, and no language
Refactoring toward deeper understanding: the model is a living hypothesis that improves through iteration, not a specification delivered at the project start
Model-Health Loop: continuous cycle of collaborative learning, model refinement, implementation, and feedback
keyTakeaways: For complex domains, the hardest part of software is deciding what to build — not how to build it. Focus on the domain.
Ubiquitous Language forces you to name things consistently across every artifact. If the developers and domain experts use different words, the model will never be clean.
A Bounded Context is the boundary within which a single model applies. No model is universal. Trying to build one guarantees confusion.
Entities are defined by identity, not attributes. Value Objects are defined by attributes and are immutable. Knowing which is which is a first-class modeling skill.
Aggregates define a consistency boundary — everything inside must be consistent; everything outside references only the aggregate root.
Repositories abstract persistence; Factories abstract construction. Neither belongs in the domain model, but both make the model workable.
The Anti-Corruption Layer is how you integrate with a legacy or third-party system whose model is fundamentally incompatible with yours.
Strategic design patterns are for large organizations. Tactical patterns are for individual bounded contexts. Most teams focus only on tactical patterns and miss the larger problem.
Domain experts are creative participants in modeling. The best models emerge from pairing a developer with a domain expert who is willing to challenge assumptions.
CRUD architecture maps database tables to code objects. It works for simple data management but encodes nothing of the business logic or language.
The model is a hypothesis. The implementation tests it. Refactoring the model — not just the code — is the path to deeper understanding.
DDD is not overkill for small teams on simple domains. It is overkill *only if applied dogmatically to every project*. Use judgment.
whoShouldRead: Senior and staff engineers working on complex business domains — insurance, logistics, finance, healthcare
Software architects and tech leads responsible for multi-team or multi-service systems
Developers who have felt the pain of a CRUD architecture that cannot express real business rules
Engineering managers who want their teams to speak a common language with their business counterparts
Anyone working with (or designing) event-driven, microservice, or bounded-context architectures
Practitioners who have heard of DDD but worry it is too abstract or too heavyweight for their project
whoShouldSkip: Teams building simple CRUD apps or infrastructure tooling where the domain has no real complexity
Junior developers without exposure to an OO or functional language and without a sense of what a real domain model feels like
Readers looking for a prescriptive framework or step-by-step implementation guide — this book teaches principles, not recipes
People who prefer short, actionable books (the original runs 560 pages and rewards slow, patient reading)
Developers who have no access to, or interest in, collaborating with actual domain experts
